claims.title: Cross-Sell Model: 6.16 Products Per Household — Structural Revenue Per Customer Advantage
claims.description: Wells Fargo achieved a record 6.16 products per retail banking household (Q4 2013), Wholesale Banking 7.1 products per customer, and WBR 10.42 products per customer. This cross-sell ratio reflects structural revenue efficiency per customer that competitors with less diversified product portfolios cannot replicate. Higher products per household create lower marginal customer acquisition cost and higher lifetime customer value.
claims.evidence: Retail Banking cross-sell: 6.16 products/household (record, Q4 2013, up from 6.05 YoY). Wholesale Banking: 7.1 products. WBR: 10.42 products. Investment Banking revenue from Wholesale Banking customers up 22% in 2013. 16 consecutive quarters of EPS growth (Q4 2013). 11 consecutive quarters of record EPS.
claims.speaker: John Stumpf (CEO), Timothy Sloan (CFO)
claims.rationale: 6.16 products per retail banking household is the highest disclosed cross-sell ratio among major US banks. Rule04: specific quantified cross-sell metrics are verified and published quarterly. Rule11: banking industry structure rewards high cross-sell as switching costs compound with each additional product held. Each incremental product deepens the customer relationship and increases the likelihood of remaining with Wells Fargo. This is structural — driven by branch density, product breadth, and sales culture that took decades to build. Score 70% (mostly convinced) — genuine and quantified cross-sell advantage with structural reinforcement.

claims.title: Deposit Franchise Scale — 13 Consecutive Quarters of Deposit Growth While Reducing Cost
claims.description: Wells Fargo grew deposits while simultaneously reducing deposit costs for 13 consecutive quarters through Q4 2013, ending with $1.1 trillion in average deposits (Q4 2014) at a deposit cost of only 9 basis points. This combination — volume growth + cost reduction — reflects a structural deposit franchise advantage from branch density and customer trust that smaller banks cannot replicate.
claims.evidence: Deposit cost: 9 basis points (Q4 2014), declining 1bp sequentially. 13 consecutive quarters of deposit growth + declining deposit costs (through Q4 2013). Average deposits: $1.1T (Q4 2014), up $22.7B sequentially (+8% annualized). Primary consumer checking customers +5.2% YoY. Primary small business checking +5.4% YoY. Total deposits up $89B or 8% for FY2014.
claims.speaker: Timothy Sloan (CFO), John Shrewsberry (CFO)
claims.rationale: Wells Fargo's 9bp deposit cost is among the lowest in US banking — a scale advantage in a high-rate environment translates directly to NIM outperformance. Rule04: 13 consecutive quarters of simultaneous deposit growth + cost reduction is a quantified structural achievement. Rule11: deposit cost is determined by customer trust, branch density, and digital capabilities — all scale-dependent. Competitors (Bank of America, Citigroup) have higher deposit costs due to different business mix. Score 70% (mostly convinced) — structural deposit franchise advantage clearly quantified.

claims.title: 90+ Business Line Diversification — Structural Revenue Stability Across Rate Cycles
claims.description: Wells Fargo operates over 90 distinct businesses, creating a revenue portfolio with structural diversification across interest rate environments, credit cycles, and economic conditions. When mortgage refinancing declined $3.4B in 2013, double-digit fee growth in brokerage, investment banking, cards, and servicing offset the loss — enabling double-digit EPS growth despite a major revenue headwind. This is a structural advantage that monoline or less-diversified banks cannot replicate.
claims.evidence: 90+ businesses across Consumer, Commercial, Wealth, Mortgage, and International. 2013: Mortgage refi revenue declined $3.4B; offset by double-digit growth in brokerage, IB, cards, mortgage servicing. Trust/investment fees: 36% of fee income Q4 2014 (from 28% prior year). Trust/investment fees = $3.7B (record high, Q4 2014). FY2014 EPS growth: 5% on flat mortgage. 16 consecutive quarters EPS growth.
claims.speaker: John Stumpf (CEO), Timothy Sloan (CFO), John Shrewsberry (CFO)
claims.rationale: 90+ business lines with demonstrated revenue substitution (mortgage decline offset by IB/cards/WBR) is structural, not strategic. Rule06: revenue diversification at this scale is not complementary but architecturally structural — built over the Wachovia merger integration (2008) and decades of M&A. Rule11: banking industry structure rewards scale diversification as each cycle favors different revenue streams. Score 70% (mostly convinced) — demonstrably proven through 16 consecutive quarters of EPS growth across varying rate/credit environments.

claims.title: Mortgage Market Scale — $50B+ Quarterly Origination Platform Despite Structural Decline
claims.description: Wells Fargo maintained $50B in Q4 2013 mortgage originations (down from $80B in Q3 2013) despite a 38% seasonal decline — retaining the #1 US mortgage lender position through its distribution network. The ability to resize the mortgage workforce (50% reduction in retail fulfillment FTEs from peak in Q1 2013) while maintaining market leadership demonstrates scale-based operational flexibility unavailable to smaller mortgage originators.
claims.evidence: Q4 2013 mortgage originations: $50B (down 38% from $80B Q3 2013). Refi share: 32% (from 65% one year prior). Retail fulfillment FTEs reduced by ~50% from Q1 2013 peak. Fannie Mae repurchase liability resolution completed. Freddie Mac repurchase liability resolution completed. Total mortgage servicing book generates servicing income. Mortgage Banking market share retained despite volume decline.
claims.speaker: Timothy Sloan (CFO)
claims.rationale: Wells Fargo is the #1 US mortgage originator by volume, but mortgage is a commoditized market where pricing is largely index-based and margins are competed down rapidly. Rule01: high origination volume demonstrates market leadership but not a structural pricing advantage. Rule07: JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America are also major mortgage originators; Wells' lead is primarily from distribution scale (branch network) but competitors are catching up via digital channels. Score 50% (somewhat convinced) — genuine scale but commoditized market limits structural moat depth.

claims.title: Primary Checking Customer Growth — 5.2% Annual Growth, 42% Credit Card Penetration
claims.description: Wells Fargo grew primary consumer checking customers by 5.2% in 2014 — the highest growth rate since 2010. Credit card penetration among checking customers reached 42% (up from 37% a year ago). Primary checking relationships are the gateway to the full Wells Fargo product suite, with each checking customer representing an incremental cross-sell opportunity. The 42% credit card penetration target implies significant remaining penetration upside.
claims.evidence: Primary consumer checking +5.2% YoY (highest since 2010). Year-to-date household growth rate through November 2014: highest since 2010. Credit card penetration: 42% (up from 37% YoY). Credit card purchase volume: +17% YoY. Debit card purchase volume: +8% YoY. New credit card accounts: +29% YoY (Q4 2013).
claims.speaker: John Shrewsberry (CFO), Timothy Sloan (CFO)
claims.rationale: Primary checking account is the 'anchor product' in banking — customers with checking accounts are significantly less likely to switch banks due to direct deposit setup, bill payment automation, and debit card habit formation. Rule04: 5.2% checking customer growth (best since 2010) and 42% credit card penetration are quantified. Rule06: primary checking is not complementary but is the structural anchor of banking relationships. However, digital banks (Chime, SoFi) are increasingly competitive on checking. Score 50% (somewhat convinced) — strong metrics but digital disruption is an emerging competitive threat.

claims.title: 55-59% Efficiency Ratio Target — Outperforming Peer Banks
claims.description: Wells Fargo targets and maintains a 55-59% efficiency ratio, operating more efficiently than many peers. In Q4 2013, the efficiency ratio was 58.5% (down 62bp sequentially). The combination of scale-driven operating leverage and cross-sell driven revenue growth enables sustained efficiency improvement even as regulatory and compliance costs rise.
claims.evidence: Q4 2013 efficiency ratio: 58.5% (down 62bp sequentially, down ~30bp YoY). FY2014 efficiency ratio: 58.1%. Management cites: 'Even at the upper end of our target range, we are operating more efficiently than many of our peers.' Target range: 55-59%. 16 consecutive quarters of EPS growth despite rising regulatory costs.
claims.speaker: Timothy Sloan (CFO), John Shrewsberry (CFO)
claims.rationale: Efficiency ratio is a management metric that reflects cost management discipline but depends heavily on revenue mix (high-margin vs. low-margin businesses). Rule08: targeting a specific efficiency ratio is a management strategy, not a structural competitive advantage. Rule07: JPMorgan Chase also operates in the 55-60% efficiency range; other competitors are variable. Score 30% (not very convinced) — efficiency discipline is genuine but not inherently structural compared to peers with similar scale.

claims.title: 16 Consecutive Quarters of EPS Growth Through Q4 2013
claims.description: Wells Fargo achieved 16 consecutive quarters of EPS growth through Q4 2013, including 11 consecutive quarters of record EPS. FY2013 earnings: $21.9B net income, $3.89 EPS (both up 16%). FY2014 earnings: $23B net income, $4.10 EPS (both up 5%).
claims.evidence: Q4 2013: 16 consecutive quarters EPS growth, 11 consecutive quarters record EPS. FY2013: net income $21.9B, EPS $3.89, both +16%. FY2014: net income $23B, EPS $4.10, both +5%. ROA: 1.151% (FY2013). ROE: 13.87% (FY2013). Net charge-off ratio: 35bp (FY2014).
claims.speaker: John Stumpf (CEO)

claims.title: Core Loan Portfolio Growth: 14 Consecutive Quarters of Year-over-Year Growth
claims.description: Wells Fargo grew its core loan portfolio for 14 consecutive quarters on a year-over-year basis through Q4 2014. FY2014 core loan growth: $60B or 8%. FY2013 core loan growth: $39.8B or 6%.
claims.evidence: 14 consecutive quarters of year-over-year loan growth (through Q4 2014). FY2014 core loan growth: $60B (+8%). FY2013 core loan growth: $39.8B (+6%). Q4 2014: core loans +$26B (+13% annualized). Broad-based growth across C&I, mortgage, auto, credit cards. C&I loans: +$36.4B (+15%) in FY2014.
claims.speaker: Timothy Sloan (CFO), John Shrewsberry (CFO)
claims.rationale: Historical loan growth fact. Verifiable via quarterly supplements and 10-K.

claims.title: WBR Client Assets: $1.6T (up 11% YoY), Brokerage Advisory Assets $423B (up 13%)
claims.description: Wells Fargo Wealth, Brokerage and Retirement (WBR) segment grew client assets to $1.6T in Q4 2013 (up 11% YoY), with brokerage advisory assets of $423B in Q4 2014 (up $48B or 13%). Brokerage advisory assets grew via positive net flows, reflecting growing wallet share in wealth management.
claims.evidence: WBR client assets: $1.6T (Q4 2013, +11% YoY). Brokerage advisory assets: $423B (Q4 2014, +$48B or 13%). Net flows: positive. Wealth management loans: +13% YoY (6th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, Q4 2014). Asset-based fees: +12% YoY (Q4 2014).
claims.speaker: Timothy Sloan (CFO), John Shrewsberry (CFO)
claims.rationale: Asset management growth fact. Verifiable via quarterly supplements.

key_themes: Primary checking account growth as anchor product
